literal: to be done; that which is to be effected; the effect; from √kṛ + suffix -ya (gerundive)
invariant_definition: *Kārya* is the gerundive of √kṛ — literally 'that which is to be done' or 'that which is to be effected.' In technical Indian usage it has two principal senses, both indispensable. (1) In Pāṇinian *kāraka* analysis it is one designation for the *karman*-role: the effect or result the agent's action terminates upon. (2) In causal theory — and this is where the great Indian metaphysical disputes live — *kārya* is the effect-side of the *kāraṇa-kārya* relation: the produced or unfolded result whose ontological status is contested. The *satkārya-vāda* of Sāṃkhya holds that the *kārya* pre-exists in the *kāraṇa* and that production is merely manifestation (*āvirbhāva*) of what was already latent (Sāṃkhya-Kārikā 9: *asad-akaraṇād upādāna-grahaṇāt sarva-sambhavābhāvāt śaktasya śakya-karaṇāt kāraṇa-bhāvāc ca sat kāryam*). The *asat-kārya-vāda* (or *ārambha-vāda*) of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika holds the contrary: *kārya* is genuinely new, a fresh *samavāya*-bound entity not present in its causes prior to production. Vedānta inherits the *satkārya* side of the dispute and then bifurcates internally over what kind of *satkārya*: *vivarta-vāda* (Advaita — the *kārya* is only an apparent transformation of *brahman*), *pariṇāma-vāda* (Bhāskara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, with variations — real transformation of cause into effect), and *avikṛta-pariṇāma-vāda* (Vallabha — real transformation without modification). The deeper conceptual point: *kārya* is never just 'effect' — it is the metaphysically loaded ontological status of the produced, which carries with it commitments about novelty, identity, and persistence.

per_school.definition: *Satkārya-vāda*: the *kārya* pre-exists in the *kāraṇa* in unmanifest form (*avyakta*) and production is its *āvirbhāva*. Five arguments classically: from the impossibility of producing the non-existent; from the requirement of a determinate material cause; from the impossibility of universal producibility; from the fact that only the potent can produce; and from the cause-effect identity of *prakṛti* and its evolutes.

*Asat-kārya-vāda* / *ārambha-vāda*: the *kārya* is genuinely new — a fresh substance bound to its parts by *samavāya* (inherence), distinct from the parts taken severally. The whole (*avayavin*) is over-and-above its parts. This is the principal target of all *satkārya-vādins* in the post-Sāṃkhya tradition.

*Vivarta-vāda*: *brahman* is the *upādāna-kāraṇa* of the world, but the world-*kārya* is only an apparent (*vivarta*) — not real (*pariṇāma*) — transformation of *brahman*. The *kārya* is *mithyā*: experienced, transactionally real, but ultimately sublatable in *brahma-jñāna*. Śaṅkara argues for *satkārya-vāda* against Vaiśeṣika in BSB 2.1.18 en route to the *vivarta* refinement.

*Pariṇāma-vāda* in modified form: the *kārya* — *cit* and *acit* in the world — is a real transformation of *brahman*'s *sūkṣma*-state into its *sthūla*-state. The Lord is the *upādāna-kāraṇa* with respect to his body (the world), but the transformation belongs to *cit-acit-viśiṣṭa-brahman*, not to the substantive Lord per se.

Bhāskara's straightforward *pariṇāma-vāda*: world-*kārya* is real transformation of *brahman* under conditioning *upādhi*. Madhva, by contrast, holds *kārya* as real but produced from real distinct material principles under Viṣṇu's controlling causality (*nimitta-kāraṇa*); *prakṛti* serves as material cause, Viṣṇu as efficient cause, retaining the categorial difference.

*Avikṛta-pariṇāma-vāda*: *brahman* really transforms into the world-*kārya* yet undergoes no modification (*vikāra*) — analogous to gold becoming ornaments while remaining fully gold. The *kārya* is real *and* the cause is unmodified.

translator_note: 'Effect' is the standard gloss but disguises the ontological commitments. Saying 'effect' in English imports a Humean or post-Cartesian event-sequence picture; *kārya* is much more loaded — it presupposes a position on whether the produced is genuinely new (Vaiśeṣika) or pre-existed in latent form (Sāṃkhya/Vedānta). The other common error is conflating *kārya* with *karman*: *karman* is the patient-role within the act-grammar; *kārya* is the metaphysical effect-side of the cause-effect relation. They overlap (both are 'that which is to be done') but have different technical homes.
